items.stem: Forwards imply the short rate will be 3% one year from now. The central bank then cuts to exactly 3% on exactly that schedule. What do long-bond holders get from the cuts themselves?

options.text: A windfall — rates fell, so bond prices must have jumped
options.explanation: The fall was already embedded in today's prices. Delivering the path the market had priced produces no repricing gain — a bond only jumps on the SURPRISE, not on the expected.

options.text: No repricing gain from the cuts — they were already in the price; holders still earn the yield and any term premium the curve was paying
options.explanation: Right. Forwards are break-evens: gains and losses accrue relative to what was already priced. Matching the implied path means no repricing surprise — though the position still earns its carry and whatever term premium was embedded in holding longer bonds. 'No surprise' is not 'no return'.

options.text: Nothing at all — an expected path means zero economic return
options.explanation: Careful: no repricing SURPRISE is not the same as no return. The bond still pays its coupons, and the curve may have been compensating holders with a term premium all along.

options.text: Short-bond holders win, because they rolled at higher rates
options.explanation: The forward is precisely the rate that equalizes rolling short with holding long. Deliver the forward path and the two strategies tie — that's what 'priced in' means.

items.stem: The yield curve inverts: 2-year government yields exceed 10-year yields. What is the market most directly saying?

options.text: Long-term government bonds have become riskier than short-term ones
options.explanation: Same issuer — the credit risk didn't flip. Inversion is about the expected PATH of rates (and the premium demanded for duration), not about default risk.

options.text: It primarily reflects an expectation that today's high short-term rates will fall
options.explanation: Correct, and 'primarily' is the right word. Expectations are the main reading — long yields sit below short yields when the market expects the short rate to come down. Term premium and maturity-preference effects also shape the curve, so inversion isn't produced by one mechanism alone.

options.text: Bond prices are about to crash
options.explanation: If anything an inversion prices in FALLING future rates, which would lift bond prices. The gloom in an inversion is about the economy, not about bonds.

options.text: The government made an error issuing too much short-term debt
options.explanation: Supply does affect yields at the margin — but inversion is driven by rate expectations and premiums, not by a clerical issuance mistake.
